title: WAL vs rollback journal
description: WAL lets readers and one writer run concurrently and is faster, but adds sidecar files and needs checkpointing.
body: Use when choosing a journaling mode. The default rollback journal copies original pages out before a write and blocks readers and writers from overlapping. WAL instead appends changes to a -wal file that readers can ignore, so many readers proceed during a write and write throughput improves; a checkpoint later folds the WAL back into the main file. Do not use WAL on a network filesystem (its shared-memory index breaks) and remember WAL still allows only one writer. Actions: enable WAL for concurrent local access, tune wal_autocheckpoint, and keep the -wal/-shm files with the database. Validation signals include readers succeeding during a write. Risk boundary: an ever-growing WAL from a long-lived reader pinning checkpoints can bloat the file — monitor and checkpoint.
